title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Three: In which Flower-in-the-Night discovers several important facts (Fragment 3)
text:
“I don’t know what has got into you,” Jamal said. “I used to think you were normal. But mad or not, you must eat.”

“There is no question of madness,” Abdullah said. “I have simply decided to go into a new line of business.” But he ate the meat.

At last he was able to pile his 189 pictures onto the carpet and lie down among them.

“Now listen to this,” he told the carpet. “If by some lucky chance I happen to say your command word in my sleep, you must instantly fly with me to the night garden of Flower-in-the-Night.” That seemed the best he could do. It took him a long time to get to sleep.

He woke to the dreamy fragrance of night flowers and a hand gently prodding him. Flower-in-the-Night was leaning over him. Abdullah saw she was far lovelier than he had been remembering her.

“You really did bring the pictures!” she said. “You are very kind.”

I did it! Abdullah thought triumphantly. “Yes,” he said. “I have one hundred and eighty-nine kinds of men here. I think this ought to give you at least a general idea.”

He helped her unhook a number of the golden lamps and put them in a ring beside the bank. Then Abdullah showed her the pictures, holding them under a lamp first and then leaning them up against the bank. He began to feel like a pavement artist.

Flower-in-the-Night inspected each man as Abdullah showed him, absolutely impartially and with great concentration. Then she picked up a lamp and inspected the artist’s drawings all over again. This pleased Abdullah. The artist was a true professional. He had drawn men exactly as Abdullah asked, from a heroic and kingly person evidently taken from a statue, to the hunchback who cleaned shoes in the Bazaar, and had even included a self-portrait halfway through.

“Yes, I see,” Flower-in-the-Night said at last. “Men do vary a lot, just as you said. My father is not at all typical, and neither are you, of course.”

“So you admit I am not a woman?” said Abdullah.

“I am forced to do so,” she said. “I apologize for my error.” Then she carried the lamp along the bank, inspecting certain of the pictures a third time.

Abdullah noticed, rather nervously, that the ones she had singled out were the handsomest. He watched her leaning over them with a small frown on her forehead and a curly tendril of dark hair straying over the frown, looking thoroughly intent. He began to wonder what he had started.

Flower-in-the-Night collected the pictures together and stacked them neatly in a pile beside the bank. “It is just as I thought,” she said. “I prefer you to every single one of these. Some of these look far too proud of themselves, and some look selfish and cruel. You are unassuming and kind. I intend to ask my father to marry me to you, instead of to the Prince in Ochinstan. Would you mind?”

The garden seemed to swirl around Abdullah in a blur of gold and silver and dusky green. “I—I think that might not work,” he managed to say at last.

“Why not?” she asked. “Are you married already?”

“No, no,” he said. “It is not that. The law allows a man to have as many wives as he can afford, but—”

The frown came back to Flower-in-the-Night’s forehead. “How many husbands are women allowed?” she asked.

“Only one!” Abdullah said, rather shocked.

“That is extremely unfair,” Flower-in-the-Night observed musingly. She sat on the bank and thought. “Would you say it is possible that the Prince in Ochinstan has some wives already?”

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Three: In which Flower-in-the-Night discovers several important facts

vocabulary.definition: A market in a Middle Eastern country consisting of rows of shops or stalls selling various goods.
vocabulary.example: Bazaar.

vocabulary.definition: A specific word given to trigger an action or response.
vocabulary.example: If I happen to say your command word in my sleep, you must fly with me.

vocabulary.definition: The act of focusing all attention on a particular task or object.
vocabulary.example: ...with great concentration.

vocabulary.definition: To acknowledge something reluctantly or after initial denial.
vocabulary.example: So you admit I am not a woman?

vocabulary.definition: Willfully causing pain or suffering to others.
vocabulary.example: Some look selfish and cruel.

vocabulary.definition: A mistake.
vocabulary.example: I apologize for my error.

vocabulary.definition: A pleasant smell; perfume.
vocabulary.example: He woke to the dreamy fragrance of night flowers.

vocabulary.definition: A facial expression showing disapproval or concentration.
vocabulary.example: A small frown on her forehead.

vocabulary.definition: An overview or rough understanding of something.
vocabulary.example: This ought to give you at least a general idea.

vocabulary.definition: Having qualities appropriate for a hero; showing great courage or nobility.
vocabulary.example: ...from a heroic and kingly person.

vocabulary.definition: A person with a hump on their back caused by spinal deformity.
vocabulary.example: ...the hunchback who cleaned shoes.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is fair and without bias.
vocabulary.example: She inspected the artist’s drawings all over again.

vocabulary.definition: Looked at something carefully to assess its condition or quality.
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night inspected each man.

vocabulary.definition: Immediately; without any delay.
vocabulary.example: You must instantly fly with me to the night garden.

vocabulary.definition: To plan or mean to do something.
vocabulary.example: I intend to ask my father to marry me to you.

vocabulary.definition: Being generous, helpful, and caring.
vocabulary.example: You are very kind.

vocabulary.definition: Characteristic of or suitable for a king; majestic.
vocabulary.example: ...from a heroic and kingly person.

vocabulary.definition: To incline or bend from a vertical position.
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night was leaning over him.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being mentally ill or extremely foolish.
vocabulary.example: There is no question of madness.

vocabulary.definition: An artist who creates drawings or paintings on the pavement or sidewalk.
vocabulary.example: He began to feel like a pavement artist.

vocabulary.definition: The act of poking or encouraging someone gently to take action.
vocabulary.example: A hand gently prodding him.

vocabulary.definition: The land alongside a river or stream (contextual meaning in text).
vocabulary.example: She put the lamps in a ring beside the bank.

vocabulary.definition: Caring only about oneself rather than others.
vocabulary.example: Some look selfish and cruel.

vocabulary.definition: To choose one person or thing from a group, usually for special attention.
vocabulary.example: She singled out the handsomest pictures.

vocabulary.definition: A carved or cast figure of a person or animal, especially one that is life-size or larger.
vocabulary.example: Evidently taken from a statue.

vocabulary.definition: To move in a twisting or spiraling pattern.
vocabulary.example: The garden seemed to swirl around Abdullah.

vocabulary.definition: A slender, curling part of a plant that attaches to a surface for support.
vocabulary.example: A curly tendril of dark hair straying over the frown.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows great happiness at a victory or success.
vocabulary.example: I did it! Abdullah thought triumphantly.

vocabulary.definition: Not pretentious or arrogant; modest.
vocabulary.example: You are unassuming and kind.

vocabulary.definition: Not just or equal.
vocabulary.example: That is extremely unfair.

vocabulary.definition: To detach or remove by taking off a hook or fastener.
vocabulary.example: She helped unhook a number of the golden lamps.
